title: Examples
text: This page provides examples of how to load and visualize the hackathon datasets using R.

title: Examples
text:
Setup
First, load the necessary libraries:

library(sf)
library(tidyverse)
library(tmap)

title: Examples
text:
Loading Data
We will load the Super Data Zones (SDZ) and the filtered Origin-Destination (OD) data.

# Ensure the data directory exists and contains the files
zones = read_sf("data/zones_sdz.gpkg")
od = read_csv("data/od_ni_open_filtered.csv")

title: Examples
text:
Visualizing Zones
Let’s take a look at the Super Data Zones:

qtm(zones)

title: Examples
text:
Analysing Commuting Flows
We can join the OD data to the zones to see which areas are the most popular destinations for commuters.

# Aggregate total commuters by destination zone
dest_counts = od |> 
  group_by(workplace_area_code) |> 
  summarise(total_commuters = sum(count, na.rm = TRUE))

# Join back to zones
zones_joined = zones |> 
  left_join(dest_counts, by = c("sdz2021_cd" = "workplace_area_code"))

# Map the results
tm_shape(zones_joined) +
  tm_polygons("total_commuters", 
              style = "quantile", 
              title = "Total Commuters (Destination)",
              palette = "Viridis") +
  tm_layout(main.title = "Commuting Destinations in NI",
            frame = FALSE)




── tmap v3 code detected ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────


[v3->v4] `tm_polygons()`: instead of `style = "quantile"`, use fill.scale =
`tm_scale_intervals()`.
ℹ Migrate the argument(s) 'style', 'palette' (rename to 'values') to
  'tm_scale_intervals(<HERE>)'
[v3->v4] `tm_polygons()`: migrate the argument(s) related to the legend of the
visual variable `fill` namely 'title' to 'fill.legend = tm_legend(<HERE>)'
[v3->v4] `tm_layout()`: use `tm_title()` instead of `tm_layout(main.title = )`
[cols4all] color palettes: use palettes from the R package cols4all. Run
`cols4all::c4a_gui()` to explore them. The old palette name "Viridis" is named
"viridis" (in long format "matplotlib.viridis")

title: Examples
text:
Route Networks
Finally, let’s visualize a snippet of the fastest route network.

# Loading a subset for faster rendering
route_network = read_sf("data/route_network_fastest.gpkg")
qtm(head(route_network, 1000))

title: Data-Driven Transport Planning Hackathon
text:
Welcome to the Data-Driven Transport Planning Hackathon!
Presented by the team behind the Propensity to Cycle Tool for Northern Ireland (PCTNI), this afternoon session is an open-ended challenge to develop data analysis, visualisation, or prototype tool ideas that help make transport planning more data-driven.

title: Data-Driven Transport Planning Hackathon
text:
The Challenge
Develop a tool, visualization, or analysis that addresses a transport planning problem. While we provide datasets from the PCTNI project, your hackathon project does not have to be focused on cycling.
Ideas for inspiration:

High-resolution accessibility mapping.
Visualizing multimodal origin-destination flows.
Predicting the impact of new transport infrastructure.
Identifying gaps in existing data.

title: Data-Driven Transport Planning Hackathon
text:
External Datasets
Some suggestions for datasets:
Translink Bus Stops - Translink Bus Stop List - Dataset - Open Data NI
Potholes (up to 2025) - Pothole Enquiries - Dataset - Open Data NI
Railway Stations (population with a walk or drive distance) - Northern Ireland Railways Stations - Dataset - Open Data NI
Traffic Count Data (up to 2023 but later is available) Northern Ireland Traffic Count Data - Dataset - Open Data NI
Road Traffic Collision Statistics – collision data can be linked to vehicle and casualty data (available 2013-2024) Police Recorded Injury Road Traffic Collision Statistics Northern Ireland 2024 - Dataset - Open Data NI
Census 2021 origin-destination place of work tables (UK) | Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency – for OD flows from 2021 census for NI – available at Census Data Zone.

title: Data-Driven Transport Planning Hackathon
text:
PCTNI Hackathon Datasets
These datasets are provided as part of the hackathon and can be downloaded from the v0.1.0 Release.

Geography and Demand

Zones (SDZ 2021): 2021 Super Data Zones for Northern Ireland (zones_sdz.gpkg).
Origin-Destination (OD) Data: Filtered 2021 Census OD data for workplace destinations (od_ni_open_filtered.csv).



Route Networks

Fastest Route Network: Network representing the fastest routes (route_network_fastest.gpkg).
Quietest Route Network: Network representing the quietest routes (route_network_quietest.gpkg).
Processed Cycle Network: A cleaned and processed version of the cycle network (cycle_net_processed.gpkg).



High-Resolution Segments
The following files contain high-resolution route segments, available in both GeoPackage (.gpkg) and R Data (.Rds) formats: - Fastest Segments: Aug 2025, 18 Aug 2025, 19 Aug 2025. - Quietest Segments: Aug 2025, 18 Aug 2025, 19 Aug 2025.

title: Data-Driven Transport Planning Hackathon
text:
Pre-event homework
To get a head start, we recommend that you try importing and visualising the Core Hackathon Datasets before the event. This will help you familiarise yourself with the data structure and ensure your technical setup is working correctly.

title: Data-Driven Transport Planning Hackathon
text:
Participation

Work individually or in teams.
Use any tools or languages (R, Python, JavaScript, etc.).
Focus on reproducibility and clear communication.

title: Data-Driven Transport Planning Hackathon
text:
Agenda (Monday 18th May)



Time
Activity




14:00 - 14:45
Getting started with the data and challenge


14:45 - 15:00
Break


15:00 - 16:30
Hackathon Session


16:30 - 17:00
Presentation of results



See the Prerequisites page to get ready!

title: Hackathon Ideas
text: Welcome to the Hackathon Ideas page! Below are suggestions across multiple categories to inspire your project. Remember: your hackathon project does not have to be focused on cycling – these ideas can be adapted to any transport planning problem.

title: Hackathon Ideas
text:
Category 1: Accessibility & Network Analysis

1. Multi-Modal Accessibility Dashboard
Combine bus stops, railway stations, and cycling routes to map accessibility to key destinations. Use Census OD data to identify areas with poor connectivity to employment centers.

Tools: R (e.g. sf/tmap) or Python (e.g. leafmap) for interactive visualization
Data: Zones, Bus Stops, Railway Stations, route networks
Key Questions: Which areas lack access to jobs? How would new infrastructure improve connectivity?



2. Last-Mile Connectivity Analyzer
Identify gaps between cycling routes and public transport hubs. Calculate walking distance from homes to nearest transit and cycling infrastructure.

Data: Cycle networks, Bus Stops, Railway Stations, Zones
Key Questions: Where do walking routes to transit fall short? What fills the last-mile gap?

title: Hackathon Ideas
text:
Category 2: Origin-Destination & Demand Analysis

3. Commute Pattern Discovery Tool
Visualize and analyze Census OD flows at different distance bands. Identify which origin-destination pairs would benefit most from cycling/transit infrastructure.

Data: od_ni_open_filtered.csv, zones_sdz.gpkg
Key Questions: Where do most commutes happen? What distances dominate?



4. Multimodal OD Flow Visualization
Create animated/interactive maps showing OD flows, with potential to estimate modal shift based on proximity to route networks.

Tools: JavaScript (D3.js, Mapbox), Python (e.g. folium), or Shiny/Streamlit
Data: OD, route networks, zones
Key Questions: Which corridors could shift to active travel? What’s the realistic potential?

title: Hackathon Ideas
text:
Category 3: Infrastructure Planning & Impact Assessment

5. Pothole & Road Quality Impact Tool
Map potholes against existing cycle routes and proposed connections. Calculate the percentage of route network affected by maintenance needs.

Data: Pothole Enquiries, route networks, zones
Key Questions: How does road condition affect route viability? What maintenance is needed first?



6. Traffic Collision Risk Mapping
Overlay collision statistics (2013-2024) on proposed cycling routes. Identify high-risk corridors for safer routing recommendations.

Tools: R or Python with spatial analysis
Data: Collision Statistics, route networks, zones
Key Questions: Which routes are safest? Where do collisions cluster? How can design reduce risk?



7. New Infrastructure Impact Simulator (advanced)
With reference to the app at npw.scot and the source code at github.com/nptscot/npw, try deploying a version of the tool to Northern Ireland or sketch out a design of a tool that estimates potential modal shift from a proposed new cycling/bus route. Use Census OD data to quantify who could benefit.

Data: OD, route networks, zones
Key Questions: What’s the user base for a new route? How many car trips could convert?

title: Hackathon Ideas
text:
Category 4: Data Gaps & Quality Analysis

8. Open Data Gap Identifier
Audit which areas have incomplete coverage in bus stops, railway stations, cycle routes, traffic monitoring, and pothole reporting. Create a “data completeness” heatmap.

Data: All external datasets
Key Questions: Where is data sparse? What’s missing for better planning?

title: Hackathon Ideas
text:
Category 5: Decision Support Tools

9. Exploring the results in the PCTNI.org web application
Explore the data presented in the PCTNI.org web application and explore either the policy implications or identify potential improvements to the user experience, visualisation, or data presentation. This could be a quick win for the hackathon and a chance to contribute directly to the project and prioritisation of investment.

title: Hackathon Ideas
text:
Category 6: Visualizations & Communication

10. Before/After Infrastructure Impact Visualization
Using the Ohsome API, create a visualization that shows how the cycle network has evolved over time in a specific area. This could be a compelling way to communicate the impact of infrastructure investment.

title: Hackathon Ideas
section: 💡 Quick Reference Guide by Team Profile
text:
💡 Quick Reference Guide by Team Profile








Team Profile
Best Ideas
Recommended Tools

3, 7, 9

R (sf, tmap, stplanr)

title: Hackathon Ideas
text:
🎯 Tips for Success

Start Small: Pick one core visualization or analysis and iterate.
Tell a Story: Don’t just show data – explain what it means for transport planning.
Use the Data: Leverage the provided datasets (zones, OD flows, route networks).
Iterate Fast: Use the 90-minute session to test, refine, and improve.
Document Clearly: Show your code, explain your assumptions, share results reproducibly.

Good luck! 🚀

title: Exercises
text: Follow these exercises to get familiar with the Northern Ireland (NI) spatial datasets and how to use them for transport analysis.

title: Exercises
text:
Guided Exercises

01-loading-data.R: Learn how to load Super Data Zones (SDZ) and Origin-Destination (OD) data, and join them to visualize commuting destinations.
02-flow-analysis.R: Convert raw OD data into spatial flows (lines), calculate distances, and filter for commutable ranges.
03-advanced.R: A starting point for routing and accessibility analysis using the PCTNI route network.

title: Exercises
text:
Working with the Scripts
We recommend opening these scripts in RStudio. You may need to install the following packages first:
install.packages(c("sf", "tidyverse", "tmap", "stplanr", "od"))

title: Prerequisites
text: To make the most of the hackathon, please complete these steps before we begin at 14:00 on Monday 18th May.

title: Prerequisites
text:
1. Create a GitHub Account
If you don’t have one already, sign up at github.com. You will use this to share your code and presentations.

title: Prerequisites
text:
2. Software installation
We recommend one of the following setups:

Option A: Local R Environment (Recommended for spatial analysis)

R: Download R
RStudio: Download RStudio Desktop
Key Packages: Run the following in your R console:
install.packages(c("sf", "tidyverse", "tmap", "stplanr", "od"))



Option B: Local Python Environment

Python 3.10+
Key Libraries:
pip install geopandas pandas matplotlib leafmap osmnx



Option C: Cloud-Based (No installation required)

GitHub Codespaces: We will provide a template repository that you can launch in your browser. Note: Using Codespaces requires a GitHub account.
Posit Cloud: Great for R users who don’t want to install software locally.



Option D: Other Tools
Feel free to use any tools you are comfortable with (e.g., QGIS, Tableau, JavaScript libraries). Just make sure you can share your work in a reproducible way.

title: Prerequisites
text:
3. Data Access
You can preview and download the core datasets from the PCTNI project repository.
Specifically, try downloading: - zones_sdz.gpkg - od_ni_open_filtered.csv

title: Prerequisites
text:
4. Familiarise Yourself
Take a look at the Propensity to Cycle Tool (PCT) and the PCTNI project to understand the types of questions we are trying to answer.
